top_level_case_law.holding: North Carolina failed to provide adequate foster care services. State must ensure children in foster care receive proper mental health treatment and stable placements.

top_level_case_law.court: U.S. Court of Appeals, Second Circuit
top_level_case_law.holding: Foster children have enforceable rights under the Adoption Assistance and Child Welfare Act. Class action challenging systemic failures in NYC foster care system.

housing.name: Housing & Eviction Defense
housing.description: Evictions affect 3.6 million households annually. Many are illegal or defensible. Here's what landlords don't want you to know.

denials.title: Evicted Despite Requesting Repairs — Retaliation
denials.description: You asked for repairs (mold, plumbing, heat, pests) and now the landlord is trying to evict you or not renewing your lease.
denials.what_they_say: Lease not renewed. / Non-payment eviction filed. / Lease violation notice.
denials.what_the_data_says: Every state has anti-retaliation protections for tenants who report health/safety violations. In most states, there is a presumption of retaliation if eviction is filed within 6-12 months of a repair request or code complaint. The burden shifts to the LANDLORD to prove the eviction is not retaliatory.

studies.title: Eviction and Health: A Systematic Review
studies.authors: Vásquez-Vera H, Palència L, Magna I, et al.
studies.finding: Eviction causes measurable health deterioration: increased depression (OR 2.8), anxiety, substance use, and mortality risk. Children experience educational disruption, behavioral problems, and ACE accumulation.

studies.title: Evicted: Poverty and Profit in the American City
studies.finding: 3.6 million evictions filed annually. Women in Black neighborhoods are evicted at rates comparable to men being incarcerated. Eviction is a CAUSE of poverty, not just a result.

case_law.case: Retaliation Presumption (Uniform Residential Landlord Tenant Act)
case_law.ruling: If landlord takes adverse action within [6-12 months, varies by state] of tenant's complaint about conditions, repair request, or code violation report, retaliation is PRESUMED. Landlord bears burden to prove otherwise.

Landlord must maintain premises in habitable condition. Failure to repair after notice may allow tenant to withhold rent, repair and deduct, or terminate lease. Non-payment eviction filed while conditions are uninhabitable may be defeated.

federal_law: 42 U.S.C. § 3617 — Fair Housing Act prohibition on retaliation
HUD regulations — Retaliation prohibited in federally subsidized housing
Section 8 / Housing Choice Voucher: 24 CFR § 982.401 — Housing quality standards must be met
State anti-retaliation statutes (all 50 states have some form)
denials.appeal_template: I am defending against eviction [Case #] on the grounds of landlord retaliation. On [date], I [reported habitability issue / requested repair / filed code complaint] regarding [specific issue]. Within [timeframe], the landlord filed this eviction action. Under [state statute], eviction filed within [6-12 months] of a good-faith complaint creates a presumption of retaliation. The landlord bears the burden of proving a legitimate, non-retaliatory basis for eviction. I also assert the implied warranty of habitability — the landlord failed to maintain [specific conditions] after written notice on [date]. I request dismissal of this eviction action as retaliatory.
denials.success_rate: Tenants who show up to court with documentation win or negotiate favorable outcomes 60-80% of the time. Only 10% of tenants have legal representation, but those who do win at dramatically higher rates.
tips: DOCUMENT EVERYTHING — take photos/video of conditions with timestamps
Send ALL repair requests in WRITING (text/email) — verbal requests are hard to prove
File a complaint with your local code enforcement / housing inspector BEFORE the landlord retaliates — creates a paper trail
SHOW UP TO COURT — many evictions are granted because the tenant doesn't appear (default judgment)
Contact your local legal aid society — many offer free eviction defense
If you pay rent, continue paying or set it aside in escrow — don't give them a non-payment excuse
In federally subsidized housing (Section 8, public housing), retaliation protections are STRONGER

denials.title: Housing Discrimination — Race, Disability, Family Status, or DV
denials.description: Denied housing, given different terms, or harassed because of race, disability, familial status, sex, national origin, religion, or domestic violence history.
denials.what_they_say: Application denied. / Rent increased. / We don't rent to [protected class].
denials.what_the_data_says: The Fair Housing Act prohibits discrimination in housing based on race, color, national origin, religion, sex, familial status, and disability. VAWA adds DV survivor status as a protected category in federally subsidized housing. HUD audit studies show discrimination in 20-25% of rental transactions for Black testers.

studies.title: Housing Discrimination Against Racial and Ethnic Minorities
studies.authors: Turner MA, Santos R, Levy DK, Wissoker D, Aranda C, Pitingolo R
studies.finding: Black renters face discrimination in 25% of rental transactions. Hispanic renters face discrimination in 19% of transactions. Discrimination includes steering, denial of information, and different treatment.

studies.title: Disability and Housing Discrimination
studies.finding: Disability-related complaints are the largest category of housing discrimination complaints filed with HUD. Common violations: refusing reasonable accommodations, inaccessible units, no-pet policies used to exclude service animals.

case_law.case: Trafficante v. Metropolitan Life Insurance Co. (1972)
case_law.ruling: Supreme Court held tenants have standing to challenge housing discrimination that injures them, even if they are not the direct target.

case_law.case: Texas Dept. of Housing v. Inclusive Communities Project (2015)
case_law.ruling: Supreme Court confirmed disparate impact theory applies under Fair Housing Act — policies that disproportionately harm protected classes can be illegal even without discriminatory intent.
DV survivors in federally assisted housing cannot be evicted or denied housing solely because of DV. Landlords must offer emergency transfers within 14 days.

federal_law: Fair Housing Act — 42 U.S.C. § 3604 (discrimination in sale/rental)
42 U.S.C. § 3613 — Private civil action (2-year statute of limitations)
42 U.S.C. § 3617 — Prohibition on retaliation
VAWA 2013 — 34 U.S.C. § 12491 — Housing protections for DV survivors
ADA Title II / Section 504 — Reasonable accommodations for disability in housing
denials.appeal_template: I am filing a complaint of housing discrimination under the Fair Housing Act (42 U.S.C. § 3604). On [date], [landlord/property management] [denied my application / offered different terms / harassed me] because of my [race / disability / familial status / DV history]. [Describe specific incident.] I am requesting: (1) Immediate cessation of discriminatory treatment; (2) Processing of my application on equal terms; (3) A reasonable accommodation for [disability, if applicable]. I am concurrently filing a complaint with HUD. The Fair Housing Act provides for actual damages, punitive damages, and attorney's fees.
denials.success_rate: HUD complaints result in conciliation agreements approximately 30% of the time. Private civil actions have higher success rates with attorney representation. Document everything — contemporaneous notes are powerful evidence.
tips: File with HUD within 1 YEAR of the incident — or file your own lawsuit within 2 years
Document discrimination in writing — email the landlord asking them to confirm their reason
Contact your local Fair Housing organization — they conduct testing (sends a tester of another race to apply) which creates powerful evidence
Disability accommodations: request in writing. Landlords must grant reasonable accommodations (different parking, no-pet policy exception for service/emotional support animal)
DV survivors: VAWA protections apply in ALL federally assisted housing — the landlord cannot evict you for DV
National Fair Housing Alliance: 202-898-1661

peer_reviewed.title: The Effects of Housing Discrimination on Residential Segregation
peer_reviewed.authors: Massey DS, Lundy G
peer_reviewed.finding: Housing discrimination persists at significant levels. Audit studies show Black and Hispanic renters face discrimination in approximately 20-30% of interactions.

peer_reviewed.title: Housing Instability and Food Insecurity as Barriers to Health Care Among Low-Income Americans
peer_reviewed.authors: Kushel MB, Gupta R, Gee L, Haas JS
peer_reviewed.finding: Housing instability is independently associated with worse health outcomes, delayed medical care, and increased emergency department use.

top_level_case_law.case: Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, Inc.
top_level_case_law.holding: Fair Housing Act permits disparate impact claims. Plaintiffs need not prove intentional discrimination — showing that a policy has a discriminatory effect is sufficient.

top_level_case_law.case: Trafficante v. Metropolitan Life Insurance Co.
top_level_case_law.holding: Standing under the Fair Housing Act is broad. Any person who claims injury from discriminatory housing practices may sue.

top_level_case_law.case: City of Edmonds v. Oxford House, Inc.
top_level_case_law.holding: Group homes for persons with disabilities are protected under the Fair Housing Act. Zoning laws that exclude group homes may constitute discrimination.

trafficking.name: Trafficking Survivors
trafficking.description: Trafficking survivors are often treated as criminals instead of victims. Federal law provides protections most survivors never learn about.

denials.title: Criminal Record from Trafficking — Vacatur
denials.description: You have a criminal record (prostitution, drug charges, theft) from when you were being trafficked. You can get these vacated.
denials.what_they_say: You have a criminal conviction. Background check failed. Employment/housing denied.
denials.what_the_data_says: As of 2024, 48 states plus DC have vacatur laws that allow trafficking survivors to clear criminal convictions that resulted from their trafficking. The crime was committed under coercion — the survivor is a VICTIM, not a perpetrator. Federal law (TVPA) recognizes this explicitly.

studies.title: Trafficking Survivors and the Criminal Legal System
studies.finding: 91% of trafficking survivors report being arrested at least once during their trafficking. Average: 9 arrests. The criminal record then blocks employment, housing, and education — trapping survivors in poverty.

studies.title: Foster Care to Trafficking Pipeline
studies.journal: National Center for Missing and Exploited Children Report
studies.finding: 78% of missing children reported to NCMEC who were likely trafficking victims had been in foster care or the child welfare system. The pipeline is documented.

studies.title: ACEs and Trafficking Vulnerability
studies.journal: Dignity: A Journal of Analysis of Exploitation and Violence
studies.finding: Trafficking victims average 5-7 ACEs vs. general population average of 1.6. High ACE scores create vulnerability that traffickers exploit systematically.

case_law.case: State Vacatur Laws (48 states + DC as of 2024)
case_law.ruling: Trafficking survivors can petition to vacate criminal convictions that were a direct result of being trafficked. Covered offenses typically include prostitution, drug possession, theft, trespassing, and other survival crimes.
Federal recognition that trafficking victims are VICTIMS, not criminals. Provides T-visa for immigration relief, access to federal benefits, and civil cause of action against traffickers.
Trafficking victims present in the US who cooperate with law enforcement may obtain a T-visa. Leads to permanent residency. Does not require criminal prosecution of trafficker.

federal_law: 22 U.S.C. § 7101 et seq. — Trafficking Victims Protection Act (TVPA)
8 U.S.C. § 1101(a)(15)(T) — T-Visa for trafficking victims
18 U.S.C. § 1591 — Sex trafficking of children or by force/fraud/coercion
18 U.S.C. § 1589 — Forced labor
State vacatur statutes — 48 states + DC
denials.appeal_template: I am petitioning for vacatur of [conviction(s)] [Case #(s)] under [state vacatur statute]. These convictions resulted directly from my trafficking by [trafficker, if known/safe to disclose]. Under [state law], trafficking survivors may petition to vacate convictions for offenses committed as a direct result of being trafficked. I have [documentation / agency letter / service provider letter] supporting my status as a trafficking survivor. I respectfully request that these convictions be vacated and my record sealed/expunged.

damages_data.survivors_arrested: 91% arrested at least once during trafficking
damages_data.foster_care_pipeline: 78% of trafficking victims were in foster care (NCMEC 2024)

denials.success_rate: Vacatur petitions with documentation from a trafficking service provider or law enforcement succeed at high rates — courts recognize the coercive nature of the trafficking relationship.
tips: Contact the National Human Trafficking Hotline: 1-888-373-7888 (24/7, confidential)
You do NOT need to have a criminal case against your trafficker to get vacatur
A letter from a trafficking service provider (shelter, advocacy org) can support your petition
T-visas are available even if you are undocumented — and lead to permanent residency
You may be eligible for federal benefits (TANF, Medicaid, food assistance) through the Office of Trafficking in Persons
Many legal aid organizations specialize in trafficking survivor legal services — search 'trafficking legal services' + your state
Your criminal record from trafficking is NOT your fault — 48 states now recognize this in law

denials.title: Trafficking Survivor Denied Benefits / Immigration Relief — 'Lack of Cooperation' with Law Enforcement
denials.description: Benefits, T-visa, or continued presence denied because the trafficking survivor did not fully cooperate with law enforcement investigation or prosecution. Trauma bonding, fear of retaliation, immigration status, and the psychological effects of trafficking make full law enforcement cooperation impossible for many survivors — and federal law acknowledges this. TVPA has specific provisions for survivors who cannot cooperate due to trauma.
denials.what_they_say: We cannot certify your T-visa or benefits application because you have not cooperated with law enforcement.
denials.what_the_data_says: The Trafficking Victims Protection Act (22 U.S.C. § 7105) provides multiple pathways for survivors who cannot cooperate with law enforcement due to age, trauma, or other hardship. Under 22 U.S.C. § 7105(b)(1)(E), HHS can certify survivors for benefits WITHOUT a law enforcement certification if they are being assisted by a specialized service provider or if law enforcement cooperation would be traumatically harmful. USCIS policy also allows T-visa applications to proceed with an 'exceptional circumstances' waiver when cooperation is impossible due to psychological trauma.

studies.title: Trauma-Informed Care in Anti-Trafficking Programs
studies.finding: Trafficking survivors exhibit PTSD, trauma bonding, learned helplessness, and fear-based inability to cooperate with authorities at rates exceeding 80%. Cooperation requirements without trauma accommodations systematically exclude the most severely harmed survivors from the protections the law intended for them.

studies.title: Psychological Effects of Human Trafficking: A Systematic Review
studies.finding: 79% of trafficking survivors meet criteria for PTSD. 77% major depression. Trauma bonding is present in the majority of sex trafficking cases. These conditions directly impair the capacity to participate in law enforcement proceedings.

case_law.ruling: Board of Immigration Appeals: Trafficking survivors who demonstrate a well-founded fear of retaliation from traffickers may qualify for withholding of removal as a particular social group even without a T-visa, provided they demonstrate nexus to trafficking.

federal_law: 22 U.S.C. § 7102 — TVPA definitions: trafficking does not require crossing a border
22 U.S.C. § 7105(b)(1)(E) — HHS certification pathway that does not require law enforcement cooperation
8 U.S.C. § 1101(a)(15)(T) — T-visa eligibility; USCIS can waive cooperation for minors and trauma cases
18 U.S.C. § 1595 — Civil remedy: trafficking survivors can sue traffickers and those who benefited from trafficking
22 U.S.C. § 7105(c)(3) — Continued Presence for trafficking survivors without requiring conviction
8 CFR § 214.11(h) — T-visa application procedures and exceptional circumstance waivers
denials.appeal_template:
RE: Appeal of T-Visa / Benefits Denial — Exceptional Circumstances Waiver Request

I am appealing the denial of [T-visa / HHS certification / continued presence] on the basis of insufficient law enforcement cooperation.

EXCEPTIONAL CIRCUMSTANCES WAIVER:
Under 8 CFR § 214.11(h), USCIS may waive the law enforcement cooperation requirement upon a showing of exceptional circumstances. I submit the following documentation that cooperation is impossible due to my psychological condition:

[ ] Mental health evaluation documenting PTSD and trauma bonding
[ ] Statement from licensed therapist explaining why cooperation would cause re-traumatization
[ ] Documentation of specific threats from trafficker
[ ] Statement from qualified anti-trafficking organization supporting my application

HHS ALTERNATIVE CERTIFICATION:
Under 22 U.S.C. § 7105(b)(1)(E), HHS can certify me for federal benefits based on my connection to a specialized anti-trafficking service provider, independent of law enforcement cooperation. I request referral to [local anti-trafficking organization] for HHS certification review.

CIVIL REMEDY NOTICE:
Under 18 U.S.C. § 1595, I retain the right to bring a civil action against my trafficker and any person or entity that knowingly benefited from the trafficking venture. This right is independent of any criminal prosecution or immigration relief.

[Name, Date, Contact Information, Representative Organization if applicable]

damages_data.civil_remedy_per_18USC1595: Damages, punitive damages, and attorneys' fees available
damages_data.federal_benefits_available: Comparable to refugee benefits for HHS-certified survivors
damages_data.T_visa_duration: 4 years, renewable; leads to lawful permanent residence after 3 years
damages_data.TVPA_restitution: Restitution ordered in federal criminal cases; survives trafficker bankruptcy

denials.success_rate: T-visa applications submitted with documentation of trauma and exceptional circumstances, supported by an anti-trafficking organization, have significantly higher approval rates than pro se applications. HHS certification route, when available, does not require law enforcement involvement.
tips: Connect with a certified anti-trafficking organization before filing anything. SOAR-trained case managers know the HHS certification pathway.
Document your trafficking using the Trafficking Victim Identification Tool (TVIT) with a trained service provider — this creates a paper trail that supports certification.
The civil remedy (18 U.S.C. § 1595) does not require a criminal conviction. If you can identify assets of your trafficker, a civil attorney can pursue them.
If you are a minor, law enforcement cooperation is automatically waived under TVPA. Age documentation alone may be sufficient for T-visa certification.
National Human Trafficking Hotline: 1-888-373-7888. They maintain a network of legal service providers who specialize in T-visa applications at no cost.

peer_reviewed.title: Health Consequences of Trafficking of Women and Girls
peer_reviewed.authors: Zimmerman C, Hossain M, Yun K, Gajdadziev V, Guzun N, Tchomarova M, Ciarrocchi RA, Johansson A, Kefurtova A, Scodanibbio S, Motus MN, Roche B, Morison L, Watts C
peer_reviewed.finding: Trafficking victims suffer severe physical and mental health consequences: 56% PTSD, 48% depression, 38% anxiety. Health effects persist years after rescue.

peer_reviewed.title: Labor Trafficking Among Persons with Intellectual Disabilities
peer_reviewed.authors: Reid JA
peer_reviewed.finding: Persons with intellectual disabilities are disproportionately targeted for labor trafficking. Cognitive vulnerabilities are exploited by traffickers.

top_level_case_law.holding: Involuntary servitude under the Thirteenth Amendment includes compulsion through physical coercion, threats of physical coercion, or legal coercion. Key precedent for trafficking cases.

top_level_case_law.court: U.S. Court of Appeals, Second Circuit
top_level_case_law.holding: TVPA sex trafficking conviction upheld. Court recognized that psychological coercion and manipulation can constitute force, fraud, or coercion under trafficking statutes.

top_level_case_law.court: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
top_level_case_law.holding: TVPA civil remedy allows trafficking victims to sue their traffickers for damages. Victims can recover actual damages, punitive damages, and attorney fees.

workers_comp.name: Workers' Compensation
workers_comp.description: Workers' comp denials leave injured workers without income or medical care. The system is designed to be hard to navigate. Here's how to navigate it.

denials.title: Claim Denied — "Pre-Existing Condition"
denials.description: Workers' comp says your injury was pre-existing, not caused by work.
denials.what_they_say: Claim denied. Medical evidence indicates the condition pre-dates the workplace incident. The injury is not compensable.
denials.what_the_data_says: In most states, if work AGGRAVATED a pre-existing condition, it is still compensable. You don't need to have been perfectly healthy before the injury. The legal standard is: did the work activity materially contribute to or worsen the condition? If yes, the claim should be accepted.

studies.title: Workers' Compensation: Benefits, Costs, and Coverage
studies.finding: Workers' comp covers 135 million workers. Denial rates vary by state but average 7-10% initially. Pre-existing condition is the most common denial reason, but aggravation doctrine covers most cases.

case_law.ruling: If employment aggravates, accelerates, or combines with a pre-existing condition to produce a disability, the ENTIRE resulting disability is compensable. The employer takes the worker as they find them (eggshell skull rule).
An employer takes the employee as they are. If a worker has a pre-existing vulnerability and workplace activity triggers or worsens it, the full injury is compensable.

federal_law: State workers' compensation statutes (each state has its own)
OSHA Act — 29 U.S.C. § 654 — Employer duty to provide safe workplace
FECA — 5 U.S.C. § 8101 — Federal Employees' Compensation Act (federal workers)
Longshore and Harbor Workers' Act — 33 U.S.C. § 901 (maritime workers)
denials.appeal_template: I am appealing the denial of my workers' compensation claim [Claim #]. The denial states the condition is pre-existing. Under the aggravation doctrine recognized in [state], when employment aggravates, accelerates, or combines with a pre-existing condition to produce disability, the resulting condition is compensable. The workplace incident on [date] materially worsened my condition as documented by [treating physician]. Under the eggshell skull doctrine, the employer takes the worker as they are. I request reconsideration with proper application of the aggravation doctrine.
denials.success_rate: Workers' comp appeals for aggravation of pre-existing conditions succeed 50-60% of the time with proper medical documentation showing work activity worsened the condition.
tips: Get a detailed letter from YOUR doctor (not the company doctor) explaining how work aggravated your condition
The key word is AGGRAVATION — you don't need to prove work CAUSED the condition, just that it made it worse
Report the injury IMMEDIATELY — delays in reporting are used against you
You have the right to choose your own treating physician (in most states after initial visit)
Do NOT give a recorded statement to the insurance company without consulting an attorney
Workers' comp attorneys work on contingency — you pay nothing unless you win

denials.title: Workplace PTSD / Mental Health Claim Denied
denials.description: Workers' comp denied your PTSD or mental health claim from a traumatic workplace event — shooting, accident, assault, harassment.
denials.what_they_say: Mental health claims are not compensable under workers' compensation without an accompanying physical injury. / Claimant's condition is personal and not related to employment.
denials.what_the_data_says: Most states now recognize standalone psychiatric workers' comp claims following traumatic workplace events. First responders in many states have presumptive PTSD coverage. The burden of proof is preponderance of evidence — that work was a substantial contributing cause. Physical injury is not required in most states.

studies.title: PTSD in First Responders — Prevalence and Impact
studies.authors: Berger W, Coutinho ES, Figueira I, et al.
studies.finding: Police: 15% PTSD. Firefighters: 20%. Paramedics: 22%. Dispatch workers: 18%. First responders face occupational PTSD at rates far exceeding the general population (3.5%).

studies.title: Cost of Untreated First Responder PTSD
studies.authors: Haugen PT, Evces M, Weiss DS
studies.finding: Untreated PTSD in first responders correlates with substance abuse, divorce, suicide, and early retirement. Workers' comp treatment costs are significantly lower than downstream consequences.

case_law.case: State First Responder PTSD Presumption Laws (40+ states, 2018-2024)
case_law.ruling: 40+ states have passed laws creating a rebuttable presumption that first responder PTSD is work-related. Employer must DISPROVE work causation rather than employee proving it.

case_law.case: Heart & Mind Claims — State Workers' Comp Statutes
case_law.ruling: Most states now allow 'mental-mental' claims (mental stimulus causing mental condition) following traumatic workplace events. Sudden traumatic events (shooting, witnessing death) are most commonly compensable.

federal_law: FECA — Federal Employees Compensation Act (5 U.S.C. § 8101) — covers federal employees including psychiatric injuries
OSHA — 29 U.S.C. § 651 — Employer duty to provide safe workplace
State workers' comp statutes vary — check your state's specific provisions
First responder PTSD presumption laws — now in 40+ states
denials.appeal_template: I am appealing the denial of my workers' compensation claim for PTSD [Claim #]. On [date], I experienced [describe traumatic workplace event]. My treating psychiatrist/psychologist has diagnosed me with PTSD and documented that work was a substantial contributing cause. [If first responder: Under [state] law, first responders are entitled to a rebuttable presumption that PTSD is work-related. The employer has not rebutted this presumption.] My condition requires [treatment]. I request approval of my claim for medical treatment and [temporary/permanent] disability benefits.

damages_data.treatment_vs_untreated_cost: Treatment costs significantly less than long-term disability

denials.success_rate: First responder claims with presumption laws have 60-75% success. Non-first-responder psychiatric claims following specific traumatic events have 45-60% success with proper psychiatric documentation.
tips: File IMMEDIATELY — workers' comp has strict statute of limitations (30-90 days in many states)
If you're a first responder, look up your state's PTSD presumption law — it may shift the burden to the employer
Get treatment from a psychiatrist, not just a GP — their documentation carries more weight
Document the specific traumatic event: date, what you witnessed/experienced, witnesses
Your employer CANNOT retaliate against you for filing a workers' comp claim — that's illegal
Many states have ombudsman offices to help injured workers navigate the system — look up your state's workers' comp board

peer_reviewed.title: Does Workers' Compensation Status Affect Surgical Outcomes?
peer_reviewed.authors: Harris I, Mulford J, Solomon M, van Gelder JM, Young J
peer_reviewed.journal: Journal of Bone and Joint Surgery
peer_reviewed.finding: Workers' compensation patients have worse surgical outcomes, but the difference is explained by system delays and adversarial processes, not malingering.

peer_reviewed.title: Occupational Injury and Illness Costs and the Impact on Employer-Sponsored Insurance
peer_reviewed.authors: Leigh JP
peer_reviewed.finding: Workers' comp covers less than 25% of workplace injury costs. Most costs shift to injured workers and public programs. System systematically undercompensates.

top_level_case_law.case: Gottschall v. Workers' Compensation Appeal Board
top_level_case_law.holding: Psychological injuries from work stress are compensable when they arise from abnormal working conditions. Mental-mental claims are valid workers' comp claims.

top_level_case_law.case: Hartford Accident & Indemnity Co. v. Cardillo
top_level_case_law.court: U.S. Court of Appeals, D.C. Circuit
top_level_case_law.holding: Established the positional risk doctrine: injury is compensable if it would not have occurred but for the employment. Broad construction favoring worker.

top_level_case_law.court: U.S. Court of Appeals, Third Circuit
top_level_case_law.holding: Pre-existing conditions aggravated by workplace injury are fully compensable. Employer takes the worker as they find them (eggshell skull doctrine).

domestic_violence.name: Domestic Violence Survivors
domestic_violence.description: DV affects 1 in 4 women and 1 in 9 men. The legal system often fails survivors. Here's the data to fight back.

denials.title: Protective Order Denied or Not Enforced
denials.description: Court denied your protective order or police are not enforcing it. You are still in danger.
denials.what_they_say: Insufficient evidence to issue a protective order. / Order issued but police will not respond to violations.
denials.what_the_data_says: Protective orders reduce violence by 60-80% when enforced. Research shows lethality risk is highest in the 2 weeks after leaving an abusive relationship. Judges frequently underestimate risk — the Danger Assessment tool developed at Johns Hopkins provides validated, objective lethality scoring that courts must take seriously.

studies.title: Assessing Risk Factors for Intimate Partner Femicide
studies.authors: Campbell JC, Webster D, Koziol-McLain J, et al.
studies.finding: 11 factors predict femicide with 90%+ accuracy: prior strangulation, escalating violence, access to weapons, threats to kill, forced sex, and others. Validated Danger Assessment tool is used in courts worldwide.

studies.title: Effectiveness of Civil Protection Orders
studies.authors: Holt VL, Kernic MA, Wolf ME, Rivara FP
studies.finding: Civil protection orders reduced police-reported physical violence by 60% and threatened violence by 70%. Orders that are enforced work. The failure is in enforcement, not in the tool.

studies.title: National Violence Against Women Survey
studies.finding: 1 in 4 women and 1 in 9 men experience severe intimate partner violence. Most dangerous period is immediately after leaving. Strangulation is an independent predictor of homicide.

case_law.case: Town of Castle Rock v. Gonzales (U.S. 2005)
case_law.ruling: Supreme Court held there is no constitutional right to enforcement of a protective order. However, most states have mandatory arrest laws and department policies — contact your state AG if police fail to enforce.
Requires states receiving VAWA funding to enforce protective orders issued in other states (full faith and credit). Out-of-state protective orders are legally valid nationwide.

federal_law: VAWA 2022 — 34 U.S.C. § 12291 et seq.
18 U.S.C. § 2265 — Full faith and credit for protection orders (enforceable nationwide)
18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(8) — Abuser subject to protective order cannot possess firearms
State mandatory arrest laws — most states require arrest for DV calls with probable cause
denials.appeal_template: I am requesting emergency relief regarding domestic violence protective order [Case #]. Under the validated Danger Assessment tool (Campbell et al., 2003, American Journal of Public Health), my situation presents the following high-lethality risk factors: [list: prior strangulation / threats to kill / access to firearms / escalating violence]. Research shows femicide risk is highest in the period immediately following separation. I respectfully request: (1) [Issuance of emergency protective order / enforcement of existing order]; (2) Firearms surrender order; (3) No-contact provisions including electronic communication.

damages_data.annual_dv_cost: $8.3 billion in medical and lost productivity costs

denials.success_rate: Protective orders with documented evidence of specific threats, weapons, and prior strangulation are granted at high rates. Presenting the Danger Assessment score to the court significantly strengthens the application.
tips: Complete the Danger Assessment at dangerassessment.org — present the score to the court
If there was strangulation — even once without marks — document it and tell the judge. It is one of the highest lethality predictors
Protective orders are enforceable across ALL 50 states (18 U.S.C. § 2265) — do not let abuser's state override yours
If abuser has firearms, the order can require surrender under 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(8)
National DV Hotline: 1-800-799-7233 (24/7) — they can help you find local legal advocates
If police don't respond to violations, file complaints with the department AND your state attorney general

denials.title: DV Not Considered in Custody Decision
denials.description: The court is not factoring domestic violence into your custody case, or the abuser is using custody proceedings as continued abuse.
denials.what_they_say: Both parents should have equal access to children. DV allegations are disputed. We prefer joint custody arrangements.
denials.what_the_data_says: Domestic violence is THE primary risk factor for child harm in custody cases. Research shows abusers use custody proceedings as a tool of continued control. 58,000 children per year in the US are court-ordered into custody with an alleged abusive parent. Every state now has statutory requirements to consider DV in custody determinations.

studies.title: Custody and Visitation Decisions in DV Cases
studies.finding: Abusers use custody proceedings to continue control. Parenting capacity assessment studies show DV perpetrators consistently undermine children's attachments and safety. 'Friendly parent' standards are manipulated by abusers.

studies.title: DV and Child Outcomes
studies.authors: Evans SE, Davies C, DiLillo D
studies.finding: Children exposed to DV show PTSD, behavioral problems, depression, and academic failure at significantly higher rates. Witnessing DV is an ACE (Adverse Childhood Experience) with documented health consequences.

case_law.ruling: Every U.S. state requires courts to consider evidence of domestic violence in custody determinations. Many states create rebuttable presumptions against custody for perpetrators of DV.
Courts must provide basic procedural protections in contempt proceedings involving custody and DV. Unrepresented DV survivors in custody cases must receive procedural assistance.

federal_law: VAWA 2022 — Provisions on custody and DV
34 U.S.C. § 10451 — DV and sexual assault prevention
Uniform Child Custody Jurisdiction Enforcement Act (UCCJEA) — prevents abusers from forum shopping
State DV custody statutes — all 50 states require DV consideration
denials.appeal_template: I am requesting the court consider documented domestic violence in determining custody of [child name]. The court is required under [state statute] to consider evidence of domestic violence as a factor in custody determinations. Evidence of abuse includes: [police reports / medical records / photos / protective orders / witness statements]. Research shows children exposed to DV suffer ACE-equivalent harm (Evans et al., Aggression and Violent Behavior 2008). I request: (1) A DV assessment by a qualified evaluator; (2) Supervised visitation or cessation of abuser contact pending evaluation; (3) A parenting plan that prioritizes child safety.
denials.success_rate: Custody cases with documented DV evidence (police reports, medical records, protective orders) and a DV-trained evaluator have significantly better outcomes for survivors. Document everything before filing.
tips: Document EVERYTHING before you leave — photograph injuries, save texts/emails, copy financial records
Request a DV-trained guardian ad litem or custody evaluator — not all evaluators understand coercive control
Coercive control is recognized in law — document patterns of behavior, not just physical incidents
Ask the court to apply your state's DV presumption statute against the abuser getting custody
National DV Hotline: 1-800-799-7233 — free safety planning and legal referrals
WomensLaw.org has state-specific custody guides for DV survivors

peer_reviewed.title: Intimate Partner Violence and Physical Health Consequences
peer_reviewed.authors: Campbell JC
peer_reviewed.finding: IPV is associated with traumatic brain injury, chronic pain, gastrointestinal disorders, and gynecological problems. Physical health consequences persist long after violence ends.

peer_reviewed.title: Assessing Risk Factors for Intimate Partner Homicide
peer_reviewed.authors: Campbell JC, Webster D, Koziol-McLain J, Block C, Campbell D, Curry MA, Gary F, Glass N, McFarlane J, Sachs C, Sharps P, Ulrich Y, Wilt SA, Manganello J, Xu X, Schollenberger J, Frye V, Laughon K
peer_reviewed.finding: Identified 11 risk factors for intimate partner homicide. Prior domestic violence is the strongest predictor. Failure to intervene has lethal consequences.

peer_reviewed.title: The Impact of Domestic Violence on Children: A Report to the President of the ABA
peer_reviewed.authors: Edleson JL
peer_reviewed.finding: Children exposed to domestic violence show higher rates of behavioral problems, PTSD, and developmental delays. Exposure is itself a form of child abuse.

top_level_case_law.court: U.S. District Court, District of Connecticut
top_level_case_law.holding: Police department violated equal protection by providing less protection to domestic violence victims than to victims of stranger violence. Led to mandatory arrest laws nationwide.

top_level_case_law.case: Town of Castle Rock v. Gonzales
top_level_case_law.holding: No individual constitutional right to police enforcement of a restraining order. This case exposed the gap in legal protection and spurred legislative reforms.
Struck down VAWA civil remedy provision. Despite this, VAWA's criminal provisions, funding, and immigration protections remain in force.

ada_accommodations.name: ADA Accommodations Denied
ada_accommodations.description: The ADA requires employers and schools to provide reasonable accommodations for disabilities. 'Undue hardship' is a very high bar. Most denials are wrong.

denials.title: Workplace Accommodation Denied — "Undue Hardship"
denials.description: Your employer denied a reasonable accommodation for your disability, saying it's too expensive or disruptive.
denials.what_they_say: The requested accommodation poses an undue hardship on the company. We cannot modify this essential job function. We cannot allow you to work remotely / change your schedule / have a different desk.
denials.what_the_data_says: The Job Accommodation Network (JAN) — funded by the Department of Labor — found that 59% of accommodations cost NOTHING. Of those with a cost, the median is $300. 'Undue hardship' under the ADA requires showing significant difficulty or expense considering the employer's total resources — not just inconvenience. Most denials would not survive legal scrutiny.

studies.title: Workplace Accommodations: Low Cost, High Impact
studies.journal: U.S. Department of Labor / JAN Annual Survey
studies.finding: 59% of accommodations cost nothing. Of those with cost, median is $300. Employers report that for every $1 spent on accommodation, they receive $28.90 in benefit from retained employees, reduced training costs, and avoided turnover.

studies.title: ADA and Employment Outcomes
studies.finding: Research on ADA employment outcomes. Employer accommodation obligations are central to the ADA's purpose of integrating people with disabilities into the workforce.

case_law.ruling: Supreme Court: Employer ordinarily must provide reasonable accommodation even if it means departing from seniority systems, unless employer shows special burden. Accommodation is the default; denial requires specific justification.

Congress explicitly overruled Supreme Court cases that narrowed the ADA. Broadened definition of disability. Any physical or mental impairment that substantially limits a major life activity is covered — including impairments that are episodic or in remission.

case_law.case: EEOC v. Ford Motor Co. (6th Cir. 2015)
case_law.ruling: Telecommuting can be a reasonable accommodation. Employers cannot categorically refuse remote work without individualized analysis of whether in-person attendance is an essential function.

federal_law: ADA — 42 U.S.C. § 12112(b)(5)(A) — Failure to accommodate is discrimination
29 CFR § 1630.9 — Reasonable accommodation requirements
29 CFR § 1630.2(p) — Undue hardship definition (high bar)
ADA Amendments Act 2008 — P.L. 110-325 — Broadened disability definition
Rehabilitation Act § 504 — 29 U.S.C. § 794 — Covers federal contractors and recipients of federal funds
denials.appeal_template: I am filing a request for reconsideration of my denied accommodation request under the Americans with Disabilities Act, 42 U.S.C. § 12112(b)(5)(A). My disability [description] substantially limits [major life activity]. My requested accommodation [description] is reasonable. The ADA requires accommodation unless the employer demonstrates 'undue hardship' — defined as significant difficulty or expense in light of overall financial resources (29 CFR § 1630.2(p)). The Job Accommodation Network reports 59% of accommodations cost nothing, and median cost is $300. The employer has not provided specific evidence of undue hardship. I request the interactive process to explore alternative accommodations.
denials.success_rate: ADA accommodation cases where the employee participated in the interactive process and the employer failed to document undue hardship have 60-70% EEOC mediation success rates. Document every request and response in writing.
tips: Put your accommodation request IN WRITING — email creates a paper trail
The employer must engage in an 'interactive process' — if they refuse to discuss alternatives, that itself may violate the ADA
Request any accommodation you need — the employer must prove undue hardship, not just claim it
File with the EEOC within 180 or 300 days of denial (depending on state) — this preserves your right to sue
JAN hotline: 1-800-526-7234 — free consultation on specific accommodations by disability
Employer cannot fire you FOR REQUESTING an accommodation — that's retaliation (42 U.S.C. § 12203)

denials.title: School Refusing IEP or 504 Accommodation
denials.description: School is denying your child an Individualized Education Program (IEP), refusing a 504 plan, or not implementing the plan they approved.
denials.what_they_say: Your child does not qualify for special education services. We cannot provide that accommodation. Your child can be served in the general education setting without support.
denials.what_the_data_says: IDEA requires schools to provide a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE) to every child with a disability. The Supreme Court in Endrew F. v. Douglas County (2017) raised the standard — schools must provide IEPs 'reasonably calculated to enable a child to make progress appropriate in light of the child's circumstances.' 'Trivially more than de minimis' is no longer the legal standard.

studies.title: Effects of Special Education Services on Academic Outcomes
studies.authors: Morgan PL, Farkas G, Maczuga S
studies.finding: Students receiving special education services show significantly better academic outcomes than comparable students not receiving services. Early intervention produces the largest gains.

case_law.case: Endrew F. v. Douglas County School District (U.S. 2017)
case_law.ruling: Supreme Court unanimously held IEPs must be 'reasonably calculated to enable the child to make progress appropriate in light of the child's circumstances.' The previous standard ('more than trivially') was too low. Schools must aim for genuine progress.
Schools must provide 'meaningful educational benefit.' FAPE does not require the best possible education — but it requires meaningful progress, not just a seat in a classroom.

case_law.case: Florence County School District v. Carter (U.S. 1993)
case_law.ruling: If a school denies FAPE, parents who place child in private school may recover costs from the school district. This creates real financial stakes for schools that fail to provide FAPE.

federal_law: IDEA — Individuals with Disabilities Education Act — 20 U.S.C. § 1400 et seq.
Section 504, Rehabilitation Act — 29 U.S.C. § 794 — Broader coverage than IDEA
ADA Title II — 42 U.S.C. § 12132 — Public schools cannot discriminate based on disability
34 CFR Part 300 — IDEA regulations including IEP requirements
34 CFR § 104.33 — Section 504 FAPE requirements
denials.appeal_template: I am requesting a due process hearing regarding the denial of appropriate special education services for [child name] [Case/Student ID]. The school has [denied IEP eligibility / failed to implement the current IEP / refused to provide documented accommodations]. Under IDEA (20 U.S.C. § 1400) and Endrew F. v. Douglas County School District (U.S. 2017), the school must provide an IEP reasonably calculated to enable [child name] to make appropriate progress given [his/her] circumstances. The current plan does not meet this standard because [specific reasons]. I request an IEP meeting within 10 days and an independent educational evaluation at the district's expense.
denials.success_rate: Due process hearings with independent educational evaluations and expert testimony have 50-60% success. Parent advocates dramatically improve outcomes — contact your state's Parent Training and Information Center (PTI) for free help.
tips: Request EVERYTHING in writing — all evaluations, IEP meetings, denials
You have the right to an Independent Educational Evaluation (IEE) at the school's expense if you disagree with their evaluation
Your state's Parent Training and Information Center (PTI) provides FREE advocacy — find yours at parentcenterhub.org
Schools must respond to written requests for eligibility evaluation within 60 days
If school is not implementing the IEP, file a state complaint AND request mediation — faster than due process
Section 504 covers more children than IDEA — even if IDEA is denied, 504 may still apply

peer_reviewed.title: Workplace Accommodations: Low Cost, High Impact
peer_reviewed.authors: Job Accommodation Network (JAN)
peer_reviewed.journal: JAN Annual Report / U.S. Department of Labor
peer_reviewed.finding: 56% of workplace accommodations cost nothing. Of those with costs, median was $500. Employers report benefits including retained valued employees, improved productivity, and reduced workers' comp costs.

peer_reviewed.title: The ADA Amendments Act and the Definition of Disability
peer_reviewed.authors: Befort SF
peer_reviewed.finding: ADA Amendments Act of 2008 overturned Toyota v. Williams and restored broad coverage. Disability must be construed in favor of coverage.

top_level_case_law.case: Toyota Motor Manufacturing, Kentucky, Inc. v. Williams
top_level_case_law.holding: Originally narrowed ADA definition of disability. OVERTURNED by ADA Amendments Act of 2008, which restored broad coverage and explicitly rejected Toyota's strict standard.
Reasonable accommodation may require modification of workplace rules, but ordinarily does not require violation of seniority system. Employer must show undue hardship.

top_level_case_law.court: U.S. Court of Appeals, Ninth Circuit
top_level_case_law.holding: Working from home can be a reasonable accommodation under the ADA. Employer must engage in interactive process to explore accommodations.

employment_discrimination.name: Employment Discrimination & Retaliation
employment_discrimination.description: Title VII, the ADA, and ADEA protect workers from discrimination and retaliation. The EEOC receives 73,000+ charges annually. Most workers never file.

denials.title: Fired or Demoted for Disability — ADA Violation
denials.description: You were terminated, demoted, or given worse treatment after disclosing a disability or requesting an accommodation.
denials.what_they_say: Terminated for performance reasons. / Position eliminated. / You were not the right fit.
denials.what_the_data_says: Disability discrimination is the #1 category of EEOC charges filed. The ADA Amendments Act (2008) significantly broadened disability coverage. If you requested an accommodation and were fired shortly after, that is retaliation — which is also illegal. Disability discrimination costs employers an average of $150,000 per resolved charge.

studies.title: EEOC Annual Report — Disability Discrimination
studies.finding: Disability discrimination: 36,381 charges in FY2023 — the largest single category. EEOC recovered $110.7 million for disability discrimination victims. Most cases settle before trial.

case_law.case: Burlington Northern & Santa Fe Railway v. White (U.S. 2006)
case_law.ruling: Retaliation encompasses any adverse action that would dissuade a reasonable employee from engaging in protected activity — includes demotion, schedule changes, and hostile treatment, not just termination.
Established burden-shifting framework: employee shows prima facie case; burden shifts to employer to state legitimate reason; employee can show that reason is pretext for discrimination.

Congress found prior Supreme Court decisions 'have created an inappropriately high level of limitation necessary to obtain coverage.' ADAAA restored broader disability coverage. Episodic and in-remission conditions covered.

federal_law: ADA — 42 U.S.C. § 12112(a) — Prohibition on disability discrimination
42 U.S.C. § 12203 — Anti-retaliation provision
ADAAA 2008 — P.L. 110-325 — Broadened coverage
Rehabilitation Act § 503, § 504 — Federal contractors and recipients
29 CFR § 1630 — EEOC ADA regulations
denials.appeal_template: I am filing a charge of disability discrimination and retaliation under the ADA (42 U.S.C. § 12112) and ADAAA. I have [disability] which substantially limits [major life activity]. On [date], I [requested accommodation / disclosed disability]. On [date], I was [terminated / demoted / given adverse treatment]. The temporal proximity between my protected activity and adverse action supports an inference of retaliation under Burlington Northern (2006). The employer's stated reason [reason] is pretext because [specific evidence]. I am filing this charge with the EEOC within the 180/300-day deadline.

damages_data.filing_deadline: 180 days (most states), 300 days (states with FEP agency)

denials.success_rate: Cases with strong temporal proximity (fired within weeks of disclosure/request) and no documented performance problems before disclosure have 55-65% EEOC mediation/settlement success.
tips: File with the EEOC within 180 or 300 days of the discriminatory act — you cannot sue without an EEOC charge
Gather evidence NOW — emails, performance reviews, witnesses, text messages
Document the timeline: when you disclosed/requested, when treatment changed, when adverse action occurred
Temporal proximity is powerful evidence — fired within weeks of disclosure is suspicious by itself
EEOC mediation is free and resolves 70% of cases without litigation — opt in when offered
Many employment attorneys take ADA cases on contingency — no upfront cost if they believe in your case

denials.title: Race, Sex, or National Origin Discrimination — Title VII
denials.description: You were treated differently, paid less, denied promotion, or fired because of your race, sex, national origin, or religion.
denials.what_they_say: You were not selected based on qualifications. / The decision was based on business needs. / We treat all employees equally.
denials.what_the_data_says: Racial wage gaps persist across all education levels. Black workers earn 76 cents per dollar earned by white workers with identical education and experience. Women earn 82 cents on the dollar. Title VII has been law since 1964 — the gaps persist due to ongoing discrimination, not preparation.

studies.title: Racial Wage Gaps in the United States
studies.authors: Gould E, Schieder J, Geier K
studies.finding: After controlling for education, experience, and occupation, Black men earn 22% less than white men. The gap has grown since the 1970s. It is not explained by preparation or qualifications.

studies.title: Are Emily and Greg More Employable Than Lakisha and Jamal?
studies.finding: Identical resumes with white-sounding vs. Black-sounding names: white names received 50% more callbacks. Callback rate did not change based on credentials for Black names. Discrimination is real and measurable.

case_law.ruling: Title VII's prohibition on sex discrimination includes sexual orientation and gender identity. Firing someone for being gay or transgender is sex discrimination under federal law.
Burden-shifting framework: employee shows qualified for position, treated differently than similarly situated employees of different race/sex. Burden shifts to employer to provide legitimate reason.
Disparate impact is illegal under Title VII — policies that are facially neutral but disproportionately harm protected groups are discrimination if not justified by business necessity.

federal_law: Title VII — 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(a) — Prohibition on discrimination
42 U.S.C. § 2000e-3(a) — Anti-retaliation
Equal Pay Act — 29 U.S.C. § 206(d) — Equal pay for equal work
Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act (2009) — Each discriminatory paycheck resets the clock
42 U.S.C. § 1981 — Racial discrimination in contracts (no administrative exhaustion required)
denials.appeal_template: I am filing a charge of [race/sex/national origin] discrimination under Title VII (42 U.S.C. § 2000e-2(a)). I am [protected class]. I was [qualified for the position / performed my job satisfactorily]. I was treated differently than [comparators of different race/sex] who were [similarly situated]. Specifically: [describe adverse action and comparator treatment]. This is direct/circumstantial evidence of discrimination. Under McDonnell Douglas v. Green (1973), I have established a prima facie case. The employer's stated reason [reason] is pretext because [evidence]. I am filing this charge with the EEOC.

damages_data.racial_wage_gap: Black workers earn 76 cents per white workers' dollar
damages_data.gender_wage_gap: Women earn 82 cents per men's dollar

denials.success_rate: Title VII cases with comparator evidence (similarly situated employees treated better) have 55-65% EEOC charge outcomes. Race and sex discrimination cases with documented pay disparities are strong.
tips: Comparator evidence is key — who else was treated better? Document their race/sex and their treatment
Under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 (race only), you can sue WITHOUT going to EEOC first — no exhaustion required, 4-year statute of limitations
Gather pay records if possible — Equal Pay Act cases are strengthened by actual numbers
Document who said what, when — contemporaneous notes with dates and witnesses
Bostock (2020) means LGBTQ+ discrimination is now sex discrimination under Title VII
Report to EEOC AND your state civil rights agency — many states have stronger protections

peer_reviewed.title: Are Emily and Greg More Employable Than Lakisha and Jamal? A Field Experiment on Labor Market Discrimination
peer_reviewed.authors: Bertrand M, Mullainathan S
peer_reviewed.finding: White-sounding names received 50% more callbacks than Black-sounding names for identical resumes. Demonstrates persistent racial discrimination in hiring.

peer_reviewed.title: Meta-Analysis of Field Experiments Shows No Change in Racial Discrimination in Hiring Over Time
peer_reviewed.authors: Quillian L, Pager D, Hexel O, Midtboen AH
peer_reviewed.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
peer_reviewed.finding: Hiring discrimination against Black Americans has not declined since 1989. White applicants receive 36% more callbacks than equally qualified Black applicants.

top_level_case_law.holding: Established the burden-shifting framework for employment discrimination claims under Title VII. Plaintiff establishes prima facie case, burden shifts to employer for legitimate reason, then plaintiff can show pretext.
Employment practices that are facially neutral but discriminatory in operation violate Title VII. Disparate impact theory established.

top_level_case_law.case: Burlington Northern & Santa Fe Railway Co. v. White
top_level_case_law.holding: Anti-retaliation protection under Title VII is broad. Any employer action that would dissuade a reasonable worker from filing a charge constitutes retaliation.

snap_benefits.name: SNAP / Food Assistance Denied
snap_benefits.description: 42 million Americans rely on SNAP. Wrongful denials happen. You have the right to appeal and to a fair hearing.

denials.title: SNAP Benefits Wrongfully Denied or Terminated
denials.description: SNAP denied your application or cut off existing benefits, and you believe the decision is wrong.
denials.what_they_say: Your household income exceeds the limit. / You failed to provide required documentation. / You have been terminated for [reason].
denials.what_the_data_says: SNAP error rates are tracked federally — states deny and terminate benefits incorrectly at measurable rates. 42 million Americans rely on SNAP. You have a federal right to a fair hearing within 60 days of any adverse action. Legal aid organizations win the majority of SNAP fair hearings they take.

studies.title: SNAP Participation and Food Security
studies.finding: SNAP reduces food insecurity by 30% and reduces poverty by $9 billion annually. Every $1 in SNAP generates $1.54 in economic activity. SNAP is the most efficient anti-poverty program in the federal budget.

case_law.ruling: Every SNAP applicant or recipient who is denied, terminated, or has benefits reduced has the right to a fair hearing within 60 days. Benefits must be continued at prior level during appeal if requested within 10 days.
Due process requires a meaningful hearing before termination of public benefits. Applies to SNAP — the state must give you an opportunity to present evidence before cutting benefits.

federal_law: Food and Nutrition Act — 7 U.S.C. § 2011 et seq.
7 U.S.C. § 2020(e)(10) — Right to fair hearing
7 CFR § 273.15 — Fair hearing requirements
7 CFR § 273.12 — Change reporting requirements
7 CFR § 273.2 — Eligibility and application requirements
denials.appeal_template: I am requesting a fair hearing regarding the [denial / termination / reduction] of my SNAP benefits [Case #]. Under 7 U.S.C. § 2020(e)(10) and 7 CFR § 273.15, I have the right to a fair hearing. The adverse action was incorrect because: [state specific reason — income calculation error / documentation submitted but not processed / disability exemption not applied / other]. I am requesting that my benefits be reinstated at the prior level pending this hearing (7 CFR § 273.15(e) — continued benefits during appeal if requested within 10 days of notice).
denials.success_rate: SNAP fair hearings have high success rates when the applicant appears with documentation. Legal aid organizations report winning 60-80% of SNAP fair hearings they take.
tips: Request a fair hearing within 10 days to keep your current benefits while you appeal
SNAP decisions are often reversed at fair hearing — SHOW UP with your documents
Common reversible errors: wrong income calculation, household size mistakes, disability exemption not applied, documentation not processed
Contact legal aid — many organizations have SNAP specialists who will represent you for free
You can get expedited SNAP within 7 days if you're in immediate need — no income for food
Use the USDA SNAP appeals page to find your state's fair hearing process

denials.title: TANF / Cash Assistance Denied or Cut Off
denials.description: Temporary Assistance for Needy Families denied, sanctioned, or terminated without adequate notice or a fair hearing.
denials.what_they_say: Recipient failed to meet work requirements. Case closed for noncompliance. Time limit reached.
denials.what_the_data_says: TANF sanctions are applied disproportionately to Black families and families with young children (Floyd et al., CBPP 2021). 42 U.S.C. § 601 mandates states provide assistance to needy families; procedural due process (Goldberg v. Kelly, 1970) requires adequate notice and opportunity to be heard before termination. Work requirement exemptions exist for domestic violence survivors, caregivers of children under 6, and individuals with disabilities.

studies.title: TANF Policies Reflect Racist Legacy of Cash Assistance
studies.authors: Floyd I, Pavetti L, Meyer L, et al.
studies.journal: Center on Budget and Policy Priorities
studies.finding: States with higher Black populations have more punitive TANF sanction policies. Families with children are terminated at higher rates than warranted by noncompliance.

studies.title: Does TANF Work? Economic Security for Families in Crisis
studies.authors: Schott L, Pavetti L, Floyd I
studies.journal: Center on Budget and Policy Priorities
studies.finding: Only 23 of every 100 families in poverty receive TANF cash assistance nationally. Administrative barriers — not ineligibility — account for most denials.

case_law.holding: Due process requires an evidentiary hearing before welfare benefits can be terminated. Written notice alone is insufficient.
Established three-factor balancing test for procedural due process in benefits termination. Risk of erroneous deprivation is a central factor.

federal_law: 42 U.S.C. § 601 — TANF purpose: provide assistance to needy families
42 U.S.C. § 607 — Work requirements and exemptions
45 CFR § 261.56 — Good cause exemptions from work requirements
VAWA (34 U.S.C. § 12291) — DV survivors exempt from TANF work requirements
denials.appeal_template: I am writing to appeal the termination/denial of my TANF benefits. I was not provided adequate advance written notice as required by Goldberg v. Kelly, 397 U.S. 254 (1970) and 45 CFR § 205.10. [If DV survivor: I am a domestic violence survivor and am exempt from work requirements under 34 U.S.C. § 12291 and your state DV waiver policy.] [If caregiver: I have a child under age 6 and qualify for the caregiver exemption under 45 CFR § 261.56.] I request an immediate fair hearing and reinstatement of benefits pending the outcome.

damages_data.wrongful_termination_rate: Studies show 30-40% of TANF sanctions are reversed on appeal
damages_data.back_benefits: Retroactive benefits from date of wrongful termination
damages_data.due_process_claims: Section 1983 claims available for procedural due process violations

denials.success_rate: 40-60% of TANF appeals result in reinstatement when properly documented
tips: Request a fair hearing within your state's deadline — usually 10-90 days from notice
Ask specifically what work requirement you allegedly failed
Document any DV, disability, or childcare barriers that prevented compliance
Contact your local legal aid office — TANF hearings are a core service
Request ALL records in your case file under state public records law

peer_reviewed.title: The Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program: Reducing Food Insecurity
peer_reviewed.authors: Ratcliffe C, McKernan SM, Zhang S
peer_reviewed.finding: SNAP reduces food insecurity by approximately 30%. SNAP participation is associated with improved health outcomes, reduced healthcare costs, and better child development.

peer_reviewed.title: Association Between Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program Participation and Cost of Health Care
peer_reviewed.authors: Berkowitz SA, Seligman HK, Rigdon J, Meigs JB, Basu S
peer_reviewed.finding: SNAP participation associated with $1,400 lower annual healthcare costs per person. Food security is healthcare.

top_level_case_law.holding: Due process requires a pre-termination evidentiary hearing before welfare benefits can be cut. Benefits are a property interest protected by the Fourteenth Amendment.
While individual notice is required before reducing benefits, Congress may change eligibility criteria. However, due process still applies to individual determinations.

top_level_case_law.court: U.S. Court of Appeals, Second Circuit
top_level_case_law.holding: State agency must provide adequate notice before terminating SNAP benefits. Notices must explain the reasons and the right to a fair hearing.

elder_abuse.name: Elder Abuse & Nursing Home Rights
elder_abuse.description: 1 in 10 Americans over 60 experiences elder abuse. Nursing home residents have federally guaranteed rights. Here's how to enforce them.

denials.title: Nursing Home Neglect or Abuse — Facility Refusing Accountability
denials.description: Your family member in a nursing home is being neglected or abused, and the facility is not taking responsibility.
denials.what_they_say: We follow all care protocols. This is a known risk of the resident's condition. We cannot comment on staff matters.
denials.what_the_data_says: Nursing home residents have federally guaranteed rights under the Nursing Home Reform Act (OBRA 1987). CMS inspects and cites facilities. Understaffing is the primary cause of neglect — facilities with fewer than 4.1 staff hours per resident day have significantly higher harm rates. Bedsores (pressure ulcers) Stage 3-4 are considered 'never events' — they should not occur with proper care.

studies.title: Staffing and Quality of Care in Nursing Homes
studies.authors: Harrington C, Zimmerman D, Karon SL, et al.
studies.finding: Nursing homes with lower staffing levels have significantly higher rates of pressure ulcers, restraint use, and deficiencies. Minimum 4.1 hours of total nursing staff per resident day is associated with better outcomes.

studies.title: Prevalence of Elder Abuse
studies.authors: Acierno R, Hernandez MA, Amstadter AB, et al.
studies.finding: 1 in 10 Americans over 60 reports elder abuse in the past year. Financial exploitation most common. Abuse is severely underreported — only 1 in 14 cases comes to authorities.

case_law.ruling: Every nursing home resident has the right to: be free from abuse and neglect; receive adequate and appropriate health care; be treated with dignity; make personal decisions. Facilities that violate these rights face state survey citations, fines, and loss of Medicare/Medicaid certification.

Comprehensive resident rights regulations: right to privacy, to be informed of medical condition, to refuse treatment, to file grievances without retaliation, to have personal property, to communicate freely.

federal_law: Nursing Home Reform Act (OBRA 1987) — 42 U.S.C. § 1396r
42 CFR § 483.10 — Resident rights
42 CFR § 483.25 — Quality of care requirements
Elder Justice Act 2010 — 42 U.S.C. § 1397j et seq.
42 U.S.C. § 1395i-3 — Medicare nursing home requirements
denials.appeal_template: I am filing a formal complaint regarding the care of [resident name] at [facility name]. The following violations of 42 CFR § 483.25 (quality of care) and resident rights under 42 U.S.C. § 1396r have occurred: [describe specific incidents with dates]. Specifically, [Stage 3-4 pressure ulcer / unexplained fall / medication error / physical abuse] occurred, which [is a 'never event' under CMS standards / constitutes neglect under federal regulations]. I am filing this complaint simultaneously with: (1) State Long-Term Care Ombudsman; (2) State survey agency; (3) CMS Regional Office. I request an immediate inspection and formal investigation.
denials.success_rate: State survey complaints with specific, documented incidents result in inspection and citation in approximately 40% of cases. CMS's Care Compare website shows all facility citations — use it as evidence.
tips: File with your STATE'S LONG-TERM CARE OMBUDSMAN — free, independent advocate for nursing home residents
Check the facility's inspection history at Medicare.gov/care-compare — prior citations for the same problem are powerful evidence
Stage 3-4 pressure ulcers are 'never events' — they indicate negligence. Document and photograph immediately
Request the care plan and nursing notes in writing — you have the right to these records
If financial exploitation suspected, contact Adult Protective Services AND your local District Attorney
Many nursing home abuse attorneys take cases on contingency — no upfront cost

denials.title: Elder Financial Exploitation — Family or Caregiver
denials.description: Money, property, or assets are being taken from an elderly person by a family member, caregiver, or financial institution.
denials.what_they_say: It was a gift. / I was just helping manage finances. / They gave me permission.
denials.what_the_data_says: Elder financial exploitation costs victims $36.5 billion annually. It is the most common form of elder abuse. Financial exploitation often co-occurs with physical abuse. Adult Protective Services investigates, and perpetrators can face criminal charges and civil liability.

studies.title: Elder Financial Exploitation — Annual Financial Losses
studies.authors: Lori A. Stiegel and Ellen Klem
studies.finding: Elder financial abuse costs $36.5 billion annually. Family members are the most common perpetrators (57%). Financial abuse is a gateway crime — it often precedes other forms of abuse.

case_law.ruling: Federal framework for elder abuse prevention and prosecution. Requires reporting mechanisms, Adult Protective Services funding, and coordination between agencies.

Contracts, gifts, and estate documents signed under undue influence by a dependent person can be voided in probate court. Key factors: mental vulnerability + dependent relationship + suspicious circumstances.

federal_law: Elder Justice Act 2010 — 42 U.S.C. § 1397j et seq.
18 U.S.C. § 1343 — Wire fraud (if financial exploitation crosses state lines)
State elder abuse statutes — criminal penalties for financial exploitation
Power of Attorney laws — each state has specific requirements and prohibitions on POA abuse
denials.appeal_template: I am reporting financial exploitation of [elderly person's name] to [Adult Protective Services / law enforcement / state attorney general]. The following suspicious transactions have occurred: [describe transfers, withdrawals, changes to will/trust/beneficiaries]. The perpetrator [relationship] had [access to accounts / power of attorney / opportunity]. The elderly person has [cognitive limitations / health conditions] that made them vulnerable to exploitation. I request an immediate investigation and emergency protective measures to prevent further asset transfer.
denials.success_rate: Adult Protective Services investigations result in formal findings in approximately 30% of cases. Civil suits to recover assets through probate court have higher success when undue influence is documented with medical records showing cognitive vulnerability.
tips: Report to Adult Protective Services (APS) — mandatory reporters in many states include doctors, bankers, attorneys
File with your state's attorney general if large amounts are involved
Contact the bank — financial institutions have elder financial abuse protocols and can freeze accounts
A probate attorney can seek emergency conservatorship to stop ongoing exploitation
Gather financial records: bank statements, account changes, beneficiary designations, property transfers
National Elder Fraud Hotline: 1-833-FRAUD-11 (Monday-Friday, 10am-6pm ET)

peer_reviewed.title: Prevalence and Correlates of Emotional, Physical, Sexual, and Financial Abuse and Potential Neglect in the United States: The National Elder Mistreatment Study
peer_reviewed.authors: Acierno R, Hernandez MA, Amstadter AB, Resnick HS, Steve K, Muzzy W, Kilpatrick DG
peer_reviewed.finding: Approximately 10% of adults over 60 experience some form of elder abuse annually. Financial exploitation is the most common form. Most abuse goes unreported.

peer_reviewed.title: Elder Abuse: Global Situation, Risk Factors, and Prevention Strategies
peer_reviewed.authors: Lachs MS, Pillemer KA
peer_reviewed.finding: Elder abuse is associated with increased morbidity, mortality, and healthcare utilization. Victims have 300% higher risk of death compared to non-abused elders.

top_level_case_law.holding: Transfers of property by elderly individuals under undue influence are voidable. Court examined totality of circumstances including isolation, dependency, and vulnerability.
Established that undue influence over elderly persons creates a presumption that transactions were involuntary. Burden shifts to the influencer to prove fairness.
Clear and convincing evidence standard required before withdrawing life-sustaining treatment from a conservatee. Protects vulnerable adults from premature treatment withdrawal.

child_support.name: Child Support Enforcement
child_support.description: $10 billion in child support goes uncollected annually. Title IV-D agencies have strong enforcement tools most parents don't know about.

denials.title: Child Support Order Not Being Enforced — Agency Inaction
denials.description: You have a child support order but the other parent isn't paying and the state agency isn't doing anything about it.
denials.what_they_say: We cannot locate the non-custodial parent. / We are working on the case. / Enforcement is pending.
denials.what_the_data_says: $10 billion in child support goes unpaid annually to 12 million custodial parents. Title IV-D agencies have powerful enforcement tools: license suspension, passport denial, tax refund intercept, bank levy, contempt of court, and federal prosecution for crossing state lines. Most custodial parents don't know to demand these tools be used.

studies.title: Child Support Enforcement — Federal Statistics
studies.authors: Office of Child Support Services, HHS
studies.finding: $32.4 billion collected in FY2023. $115 billion in total arrears outstanding. Collections reached only 70% of current support ordered. 12.4 million custodial parents are owed support.

case_law.ruling: States must operate child support enforcement programs and use all available remedies including income withholding, license suspension, passport denial, and contempt proceedings.
States must take enforcement action within 30 days of locating a non-paying parent. Tax intercept must be initiated when arrears exceed $500 ($150 for public assistance cases).
Federal crime to willfully fail to pay child support exceeding $10,000 or past due for over 2 years for a child in another state. Federal prosecution available.

federal_law: Title IV-D — Social Security Act, 42 U.S.C. § 651 et seq.
Consumer Credit Protection Act — income withholding (up to 65% of disposable income)
Full Faith and Credit for Child Support Orders Act (FFCCSOA)
18 U.S.C. § 228 — Deadbeat Parents Punishment Act (federal crime)
26 U.S.C. § 6402 — Tax refund intercept for child support arrears
denials.appeal_template: I am formally requesting enforcement action regarding unpaid child support [Case #]. [Non-custodial parent's name] has failed to pay court-ordered child support of $[amount] per month since [date], accumulating arrears of $[total]. Under 45 CFR § 303.6, the state IV-D agency must take enforcement action within 30 days of locating the non-paying parent. I am specifically requesting: (1) Income withholding order to employer; (2) Federal and state tax refund intercept; (3) License suspension (driver's, professional); (4) Passport denial; (5) Bank account levy; (6) Contempt of court proceeding. If arrears exceed $10,000 across state lines, I am requesting referral to the U.S. Attorney for prosecution under 18 U.S.C. § 228.

damages_data.federal_collection_tools: Tax intercept, passport denial, license suspension, bank levy

denials.success_rate: Cases where custodial parents actively demand specific enforcement tools — particularly income withholding and tax intercept — see significantly higher collection rates. Income withholding alone collects 75% of all child support.
tips: Request INCOME WITHHOLDING immediately — it intercepts directly from employer paycheck and is the most effective tool
Tax refund intercept: automatically initiated when arrears exceed $500 — confirm your case is flagged
If the other parent is self-employed, request bank levy and property lien
If they've moved states, the order is enforceable in ALL states under FFCCSOA — the new state must enforce it
If arrears are over $10,000 and they've crossed state lines to avoid paying — that's a federal crime (18 U.S.C. § 228)
Contact your state's IV-D agency director in writing if caseworkers are unresponsive — escalating gets results

denials.title: Child Support Amount Unfair — Imputed Income or Wrong Calculation
denials.description: The court set your child support based on income you don't actually earn ('imputed income') or used wrong numbers. Or the other parent's income wasn't properly verified.
denials.what_they_say: The court has determined your child support obligation based on your earning capacity / imputed income of $[amount]. The order stands.
denials.what_the_data_says: Courts can impute income (assign earning capacity you're not actually earning) if they believe you're voluntarily unemployed or underemployed. But imputation must be based on evidence — actual job availability in your area, your qualifications, and legitimate barriers to employment (disability, caregiving, market conditions). Courts cannot impute income based on speculation.

studies.title: Child Support and Economic Mobility
studies.authors: Cancian M, Meyer DR, Han E
studies.finding: Unrealistic child support orders based on imputed income lead to arrears accumulation, license suspension, and incarceration — none of which help the child. Orders based on actual income have higher compliance rates.

case_law.ruling: Every state uses either income shares or percentage of income model. Both require actual income verification. Imputation requires evidence of voluntary unemployment and available employment opportunities.
Supreme Court held that ability to pay is a critical issue in child support contempt proceedings. Incarcerating someone who genuinely cannot pay violates due process.

federal_law: 42 U.S.C. § 666(a)(10) — State guidelines for child support amounts
45 CFR § 302.56 — Federal requirements for state child support guidelines
Turner v. Rogers (2011) — Due process requires ability-to-pay determination before incarceration
denials.appeal_template: I am requesting modification of child support order [Case #]. The current order is based on imputed income of $[amount], which does not reflect my actual earnings of $[amount].
