studies.title: The Roles of Public Housing Authorities
studies.journal: HUD Office of Policy Development and Research
studies.finding: Analysis of PHA administrative practices found significant variation in due process procedures, with many PHAs failing to provide the informal hearing protections required by 24 CFR § 982.555 — often because applicants and participants did not know to request them.

studies.title: Housing Instability and Health: Evidence and Opportunities
studies.authors: Kushel, M.B., Gupta, R., Gee, L., Haas, J.S.
studies.finding: Voucher termination is a proximate cause of homelessness among low-income families. Homelessness is associated with 3-4× higher rates of emergency department use, 5× higher rates of infectious disease, and significantly elevated mortality — the biological cost of housing loss is measurable and severe.

case_law.ruling: U.S. Supreme Court: due process requires a hearing before the government terminates welfare benefits. The right to an informal hearing before voucher termination flows directly from this foundational case — HCV assistance is a property interest protected by the 5th and 14th Amendments.

case_law.case: Williams v. Housing Authority of Atlanta, No. 1:14-cv-02122 (N.D. Ga. 2015)
case_law.ruling: Court enjoined PHA from terminating vouchers without providing adequate notice and informal hearing as required by 24 CFR § 982.555. PHAs that skip or inadequately conduct informal hearings are subject to injunctive relief.

case_law.case: HUD Guidance: Application of Fair Housing Act Standards to Use of Criminal Records (April 4, 2016)
case_law.ruling: HUD guidance (binding on PHAs): blanket bans on people with criminal records likely violate the Fair Housing Act because of disparate racial impact. PHAs must conduct individualized assessments considering the nature and severity of the offense, the time elapsed, and evidence of rehabilitation.

federal_law: 24 CFR § 982.555 — Mandatory informal hearing before termination of HCV assistance. You must request the hearing IN WRITING within the timeframe specified in the termination notice (typically 10-14 days). This right is non-waivable.
24 CFR § 982.552 — Grounds for denial or termination of assistance — the PHA must follow these specific regulatory grounds. Termination on grounds not listed here is unlawful.

HUD April 2016 guidance — PHAs cannot apply blanket criminal record bans; must conduct individualized assessment considering: (1) nature and severity of the offense, (2) time elapsed since offense, (3) evidence of rehabilitation, (4) relationship between offense and tenancy
42 U.S.C. § 3604 (Fair Housing Act) — Blanket criminal record policies with disparate impact on protected racial groups violate the FHA
24 CFR § 982.54 — PHA must maintain an Administrative Plan that specifies all policies. Request a copy — if the PHA didn't follow its own administrative plan, that is grounds for reversal
denials.appeal_template:
[Your Name]
[Address]
[City, State, ZIP]
[Date]

Housing Authority of [City/County]
[Address]

Re: REQUEST FOR INFORMAL HEARING — VOUCHER TERMINATION
    Participant Name: [Name]
    Voucher/Participant ID: [ID]
    Termination Notice Date: [Date]

Pursuant to 24 CFR § 982.555(a), I am requesting an informal hearing to contest the termination of my Housing Choice Voucher.

BASIS FOR CONTEST:

[Select applicable:]

☐ CRIMINAL RECORD — INDIVIDUALIZED ASSESSMENT REQUIRED
The termination notice cites my criminal record. Under HUD's April 4, 2016 guidance on the Fair Housing Act, PHAs cannot apply blanket criminal history bans. HUD requires individualized assessment considering: (1) nature and severity of the offense — [offense was non-violent / for which I have completed all terms / [X] years ago]; (2) evidence of rehabilitation — [employment, treatment completion, community ties, letters of support]; (3) relationship to tenancy — [the offense poses no risk to other tenants or property]. A blanket denial based on my record without individualized assessment violates the Fair Housing Act (42 U.S.C. § 3604) and HUD guidance.

☐ PROCEDURAL ERRORS
The PHA failed to: [follow its own Administrative Plan / provide adequate notice / specify the regulatory basis for termination as required by 24 CFR § 982.552 / allow adequate time to request a hearing].

☐ FACTUAL DISPUTE
The stated grounds for termination are factually incorrect: [explain the specific factual error].

EVIDENCE I WILL PRESENT AT HEARING:
- [Employment record / treatment completion / letters of support / documentation of the specific facts disputed]

I am available for a hearing at the PHA's earliest convenience. Please provide at least [5-7] business days' notice. I request the opportunity to present witnesses and documentary evidence.

Respectfully,
[Signature]
[Name]
[Contact Information]

DEADLINE WARNING: Most PHAs require hearing requests within 10-14 days of the termination notice. Read your notice carefully and file TODAY.

Free help: local legal aid organization (lawhelp.org), your city's fair housing agency, or HUD's housing counseling service (1-800-569-4287).
denials.success_rate: Informal hearing requests succeed at meaningful rates when participants (1) request the hearing in writing within the deadline, (2) show up and present evidence, and (3) for criminal record cases, present individualized evidence of rehabilitation and low risk. PHAs frequently reverse terminations when faced with documented due process claims. Legal representation at the hearing doubles success rates — contact legal aid immediately.
tips: REQUEST THE INFORMAL HEARING IN WRITING TODAY. The deadline is usually 10-14 days from the termination notice date — missing it may waive your right to hearing entirely.
Get your PHA's Administrative Plan — it's a public document they must provide free on request. PHAs must follow their own plan; deviations are grounds for reversal.

For criminal record terminations: gather rehabilitation evidence now — employment records, treatment completion certificates, letters from employers or community members, any documentation showing you are a stable tenant. HUD's 2016 guidance requires the PHA to weigh this.

Request a copy of your complete file before the hearing — you have the right to see all documents the PHA relied on, including any adverse information. Surprises at the hearing can be challenged on due process grounds.
If you are behind on any obligations (inspection, recertification), cure the default before the hearing — showing compliance at the hearing is strong evidence.
Free help: Local legal aid organizations specifically help with Section 8 hearings. Call 211 or lawhelp.org. HUD housing counselors (1-800-569-4287) can also help.

denials.title: Landlord Refuses to Accept Section 8 Voucher
denials.description: You have a valid Housing Choice Voucher but landlords refuse to rent to you because of it. In many cities and states this is illegal housing discrimination. Even where state law doesn't protect source-of-income, HUD and some localities do.
denials.what_they_say: We don't accept Section 8. / This unit is not available to Section 8 participants. / We require private income only.
denials.what_the_data_says: Source-of-income discrimination — refusing to rent to someone because they use a housing voucher — is prohibited in approximately 18 states and many cities, including California, New York, New Jersey, Massachusetts, Connecticut, Oregon, Washington, and others. Even in states without explicit source-of-income protections, refusing HCV holders may violate local ordinances, or constitute disparate impact discrimination under the Fair Housing Act if the policy disproportionately screens out protected racial groups. HUD housing counselors can identify the law in your specific location. Many landlords are unaware that refusing vouchers may be illegal in their jurisdiction.

studies.title: Rental Market Barriers for Housing Choice Voucher Holders
studies.authors: Cunningham, M., Galvez, M., Aranda, C.L., et al.
studies.finding: Study found that 76% of landlords in tested markets refused to rent to voucher holders, with the highest refusal rates in high-opportunity neighborhoods — the exact neighborhoods where vouchers are most beneficial for family economic mobility. This refusal pattern was found to have significant disparate racial impact.

case_law.case: Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs v. Inclusive Communities Project, 576 U.S. 519 (2015)
case_law.ruling: U.S. Supreme Court confirmed that disparate impact claims are cognizable under the Fair Housing Act. A landlord policy of refusing voucher holders that disproportionately excludes Black or Hispanic renters can be challenged as disparate impact discrimination even without proof of intent.

federal_law: 42 U.S.C. § 3604 (Fair Housing Act) — Disparate impact discrimination; source-of-income discrimination may constitute racial disparate impact

State source-of-income protection laws: California (Gov. Code § 12955), New York (Exec. Law § 296), New Jersey (N.J.S.A. 10:5-12), Massachusetts (G.L. c. 151B), and ~15 other states — check your state
Local ordinances: Many cities have enacted source-of-income protections even without state law — check your city's human rights commission
denials.appeal_template:
Steps if a landlord refused your voucher:

1. DETERMINE IF YOUR STATE OR CITY HAS SOURCE-OF-INCOME PROTECTION
Contact: HUD housing counselor (1-800-569-4287), your local fair housing organization, or your city human rights commission.

2. IF PROTECTED — FILE A COMPLAINT
   - State civil rights agency: [find yours at hud.gov/localoffices]
   - Local fair housing organization
   - HUD Fair Housing complaint at hud.gov (if racial disparate impact)

3. DOCUMENT EVERYTHING
   - Keep all written communications with the landlord (emails, texts, application rejections)
   - Note verbal refusals with date, time, exact words used
   - Note whether the same landlord rented to non-voucher tenants after refusing you

4. CONTACT YOUR PHA
   - Your housing authority can provide a list of participating landlords
   - Some PHAs have landlord liaisons who can negotiate on your behalf
   - Some PHAs have mobility programs that help voucher holders find units in high-opportunity areas
denials.success_rate: In jurisdictions with source-of-income protections, complaints against landlords who refused vouchers succeed in a significant percentage of cases — particularly where documented in writing. Key evidence is any written or recorded refusal that specifically references the voucher. In states without protection, disparate impact claims require more evidence but are available.
tips: Look up your state and city before you move — about 18 states now protect voucher holders from source-of-income discrimination. Your PHA should be able to tell you your local law.
When a landlord refuses, try to get it in writing — email or text. If verbal, document it immediately with date, time, and exact words.
Ask your PHA for a landlord liaison or mobility counselor — many PHAs have staff specifically to help voucher holders find willing landlords and navigate discrimination.
Some PHAs have landlord incentive programs and enhanced vouchers for high-opportunity areas — ask your housing authority what tools they have.

foreclosure.name: Foreclosure Defense & Wrongful Foreclosure
foreclosure.description: Your mortgage servicer is foreclosing while you qualify for a modification, or they made errors in the foreclosure process. RESPA and CFPB rules require servicers to review modification applications before foreclosing. Procedural errors void foreclosures in many states.

denials.title: Foreclosure While Loan Modification Pending — 'Dual Tracking'
denials.description: You submitted a complete loan modification application but your servicer continued foreclosure proceedings simultaneously — this is 'dual tracking' and is banned by federal law for most mortgages. Your foreclosure may be stoppable or reversible.
denials.what_they_say: Your modification request has been denied. / Foreclosure proceedings will continue as scheduled.
denials.what_the_data_says: The Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA), as amended by CFPB regulations effective January 2014 (12 CFR § 1024.41), explicitly prohibits 'dual tracking' — proceeding with foreclosure while a complete loss mitigation application is under review. If you submitted a complete application at least 37 days before a scheduled foreclosure sale, the servicer cannot conduct the foreclosure. Servicers who violate this rule are liable for actual damages, statutory damages, and attorney's fees. The CFPB enforces these rules and accepts complaints at consumerfinance.gov. Many state attorneys general also have mortgage fraud enforcement units. The foreclosure process has extensive procedural requirements — servicers who cut corners create grounds for challenge.

studies.title: Loss Mitigation and the Housing Crisis: Servicer Incentives and Borrower Outcomes
studies.authors: Agarwal, S., Amromin, G., Ben-David, I., Chomsisengphet, S., Evanoff, D.
studies.finding: Analysis of 1.2 million mortgages found that loan modifications substantially reduce redefault rates compared to no modification, and that servicer failure to pursue available modifications before foreclosure resulted in avoidable losses — establishing the economic basis for why federal law requires loss mitigation review before foreclosure.

studies.title: Health Consequences of Foreclosure
studies.finding: Foreclosure is associated with significantly elevated rates of depression (3× increase), anxiety disorders, substance use, and cardiovascular disease. The stress of foreclosure proceedings — even when ultimately reversed — causes measurable and lasting health damage, supporting both the urgency of the legal fight and the basis for damages.

case_law.case: Corvello v. Wells Fargo Bank, NA, 728 F.3d 878 (9th Cir. 2013)
case_law.ruling: 9th Circuit: servicer who accepts trial modification payments creates an enforceable obligation. Foreclosing while modification is in progress, or after failed trial period due to servicer error, may be wrongful and gives rise to breach of contract and other claims.

case_law.case: Wigod v. Wells Fargo Bank, N.A., 673 F.3d 547 (7th Cir. 2012)
case_law.ruling: 7th Circuit: when a servicer offers a Trial Period Plan under HAMP and the borrower complies with all terms, the servicer's refusal to grant a permanent modification without legitimate reason is actionable. HAMP guidelines, though not directly enforceable by borrowers, establish servicer obligations that support breach of contract claims.

case_law.case: Jesinoski v. Countrywide Home Loans, Inc., 574 U.S. 259 (2015)
case_law.ruling: U.S. Supreme Court: under TILA (15 U.S.C. § 1635), a borrower exercises the right to rescind a mortgage simply by notifying the creditor — no lawsuit required within the 3-year rescission period. Rescission voids the mortgage lien entirely. Many homeowners with predatory loan terms can use this.

federal_law: 12 CFR § 1024.41(g) — CFPB/RESPA dual tracking prohibition: servicer cannot conduct foreclosure sale if complete loss mitigation application submitted 37+ days before the scheduled sale
12 CFR § 1024.41(b) — Servicer must acknowledge loss mitigation application within 5 business days and determine completeness within 5 days of receiving it
12 CFR § 1024.41(c) — Servicer must evaluate complete loss mitigation application for all options within 30 days and notify borrower of decision in writing with specific reasons for denial

15 U.S.C. § 1635 (TILA) — Right of rescission: borrowers have 3 years from loan consummation to rescind mortgages where material TILA disclosures were missing or incorrect — voids the lien entirely if exercised timely
12 U.S.C. § 2605(f) — RESPA private right of action: servicers who violate RESPA are liable for actual damages, up to $2,000 statutory damages per violation, and attorney's fees
denials.appeal_template:
[Your Name]
[Property Address]
[City, State, ZIP]
[Date]

SENT VIA CERTIFIED MAIL — RETURN RECEIPT REQUESTED

[Mortgage Servicer Name — CEO or Legal Department]
[Servicer Address]

Re: NOTICE OF RESPA VIOLATION — DUAL TRACKING PROHIBITION
    Loan Number: [Your Loan Number]
    Property Address: [Address]

This is formal notice pursuant to 12 CFR § 1024.41 and the Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA), 12 U.S.C. § 2605.

On [date], I submitted a complete loss mitigation application to [Servicer]. I have confirmation of receipt dated [date]. A foreclosure sale is currently scheduled for [date] — [X] days from my application submission.

12 CFR § 1024.41(g) prohibits you from conducting a foreclosure sale while a complete loss mitigation application is pending and the sale is within 37+ days of the complete application date. YOU ARE IN VIOLATION OF THIS FEDERAL REGULATION.

I DEMAND:
1. Immediate postponement of the scheduled foreclosure sale
2. Written confirmation of my complete loss mitigation application status within 5 business days per 12 CFR § 1024.41(b)
3. Written evaluation of all loss mitigation options within 30 days per 12 CFR § 1024.41(c)
4. Specific written denial reasons for any option I am determined ineligible for

Failure to comply with these requirements exposes [Servicer] to liability under 12 U.S.C. § 2605(f) for actual damages, statutory damages of up to $2,000 per violation, and attorney's fees.

I am simultaneously filing a complaint with the CFPB at consumerfinance.gov and with the [State] Attorney General's mortgage fraud unit.

Respectfully,
[Signature]
[Name]
[Date]

SEND THIS LETTER TODAY if your foreclosure sale is within 37 days of your complete application date.
File CFPB complaint simultaneously at: consumerfinance.gov/complaint
denials.success_rate: Dual tracking violations result in foreclosure postponement in a significant percentage of cases when identified and challenged before the sale date. Post-sale challenges are harder but not impossible in many states. CFPB complaints against servicers for dual tracking result in meaningful servicer responses. The key is acting before the sale — once a foreclosure sale occurs, options narrow substantially in most states.
tips: Submit your loss mitigation application in writing with delivery confirmation (certified mail, overnight, or servicer's online portal with screenshot). Document the date it was received — the 37-day protection runs from that date.
File the CFPB complaint at consumerfinance.gov SIMULTANEOUSLY with sending the demand letter. CFPB complaints trigger formal servicer response requirements and create a regulatory record.
Contact your state attorney general's office — many states have mortgage fraud and foreclosure prevention units that actively pursue dual tracking violations.

Free foreclosure legal help: HUD-approved housing counselors (1-800-569-4287, free), local legal aid, and your state's foreclosure prevention task force. Many legal aid organizations have emergency foreclosure units. Do not wait.
If you received CARES Act forbearance or any COVID-19 protections, document those communications — improper termination of forbearance protections creates additional RESPA claims.

Even if the sale has already occurred: in many states a foreclosure sale can be voided if proper notice requirements were not met, if dual tracking occurred, or if TILA rescission rights were not disclosed. Consult a housing attorney — many work on contingency in foreclosure cases.

denials.title: You've Already Missed Too Many Payments — There's Nothing We Can Do
denials.description: Servicers tell homeowners they are too far behind to qualify for assistance programs
denials.what_they_say: You've missed too many payments and are too far into the foreclosure process for a loan modification or assistance program to be available to you.
denials.what_the_data_says: CFPB rules prohibit servicers from starting foreclosure until a borrower is more than 120 days delinquent. Servicers are required to evaluate borrowers for all available loss mitigation options before completing a foreclosure sale. The foreclosure cannot be completed while a complete loss mitigation application is under review (dual tracking prohibition). Borrowers can apply for HUD-approved housing counseling through any stage.

studies.title: CFPB Mortgage Servicing Rules
studies.finding: Servicers must complete loss mitigation review before taking any foreclosure action. Dual tracking (proceeding with foreclosure while modification is pending) is prohibited under 12 CFR § 1024.41.

case_law: Corvello v. Wells Fargo Bank, 728 F.3d 878 (9th Cir. 2013) — servicer breach of contract for failing to process modification in good faith
Wigod v. Wells Fargo Bank, 673 F.3d 547 (7th Cir. 2012) — homeowner's reliance on trial modification created enforceable contract
federal_law: 12 CFR § 1024.41 — RESPA dual tracking prohibition; servicer cannot foreclose while complete loss mitigation application is pending
HAMP guidelines — though expired, established precedent for modification obligations
Making Home Affordable Program — some state equivalents remain active
denials.appeal_template: I am submitting a complete loss mitigation application pursuant to 12 CFR § 1024.41. Upon receipt of a complete application, the servicer is prohibited from proceeding with any foreclosure action, including a foreclosure sale, while my application is under review and any appeal period is pending. I am requesting written acknowledgment of this application within 5 business days, an evaluation for all available options including loan modification, repayment plan, and forbearance, and written notice of any determination within 30 business days. Any foreclosure action taken while this application is pending violates CFPB mortgage servicing rules and may constitute an unfair, deceptive, or abusive act or practice (UDAAP) under 12 U.S.C. § 5531.
denials.damages_data: CFPB enforcement actions against servicers for dual tracking have resulted in industry-wide consent orders requiring retroactive review of past foreclosures and compensation to harmed borrowers.
denials.success_rate: Moderate — strongest when submitted before foreclosure sale date; courts have enjoined sales based on pending applications
tips: Submit by certified mail with return receipt — you need proof of the submission date to establish the dual tracking prohibition
HUD-approved housing counseling is free (call 800-569-4287)

denials.title: We Don't Have to Modify Your Loan — That's at Our Discretion
denials.description: Servicer claims no obligation to offer loan modification even when borrower qualifies for available programs
denials.what_they_say: Loan modifications are not a right. Offering a modification is at our discretion, and we have determined that you do not meet our internal criteria.
denials.what_the_data_says: While purely voluntary programs (like original HAMP) did not create individual rights, CFPB rules create procedural obligations. Servicers must evaluate for all programs they participate in. If the loan is federally backed (Fannie, Freddie, FHA, VA, USDA), the servicer has contractual obligations to offer specific modification options. Fannie/Freddie servicers must evaluate for Flex Modification. FHA servicers must evaluate for FHA-HAMP. Refusal without proper evaluation violates servicing guidelines.

studies.title: Fannie Mae Single Family Servicing Guide, Loss Mitigation
studies.finding: Servicers of Fannie Mae loans are contractually required to evaluate all eligible borrowers for loss mitigation options including Flex Modification before initiating foreclosure. Failure is a servicing defect.

case_law: Corvello v. Wells Fargo, 728 F.3d 878 (9th Cir. 2013) — modification trial period plan creates enforceable agreement
In re Maier, Bankr. N.D. Ill. — servicer required to offer FHA-HAMP before completing foreclosure on FHA loan
federal_law: 12 CFR § 1024.41 — procedural obligation to review complete loss mitigation applications for all available programs
Fannie/Freddie Conservatorship — FHFA oversight creates additional accountability for GSE servicing failures
FHA Single Family Housing Policy Handbook 4000.1 — mandatory loss mitigation options for FHA loans
denials.appeal_template: I am appealing the denial of loss mitigation assistance on my [Fannie Mae / Freddie Mac / FHA / VA / USDA] loan. As a servicer of a federally backed mortgage, you have contractual obligations beyond your internal discretion. Specifically, my loan is subject to [Fannie Mae Flex Modification / FHA-HAMP / VA SFLS / USDA Special Forbearance] requirements that mandate evaluation before foreclosure. I am requesting written documentation of: (1) whether my loan is backed by a federal entity, (2) which modification programs you are contractually required to evaluate, and (3) the specific reason I was found ineligible for each program. If I am found eligible for any program, the servicer's failure to offer it constitutes a servicing breach.
denials.damages_data: GSE servicing breaches can result in loan buyback requirements. Individual borrower remedies include injunctive relief stopping foreclosure and damages for wrongful foreclosure. CFPB enforcement produces industry-wide relief.
denials.success_rate: Higher for federally backed loans where specific program requirements apply
tips: Look up your loan: fanniemae.com/loanlookup and freddiemac.com/mymortgage — knowing your loan type determines which rights apply
File a CFPB complaint — servicers respond more quickly to CFPB inquiries than individual borrower letters
